root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

random variables

Discrete and absolutely continuous measures

Let $(\calx,\mfm)$ be a measurable space and $C\in \mfm$ a countable set. If we write $\mfp(C)$ for the power set of $C$, then the inclusion map

\begin{equation}\label{inc-eqn} \iota:(C,\mfp(C)) \to (\calx, \mfm) \end{equation}

is measurable. We write $\gamma_C$ for the pushforward of the counting measure from $(C,\mfp(C))$, which we call the counting measure concentrated on $C$.1

Definition. Let $\mu$ be a measure on a measurable space $(\calx,\mfm)$ and let $C\in \mfm$ be a countable set. We shall say $\mu$ is discrete if there exists a measurable function $f:\calx \to [0,\infty)$ such that \begin{equation}\notag \mu(E) = \int_E f \dx{\gamma_C} \end{equation} for all $E\in \mfm$. In this case, $f$ is called a density function of $\mu$, and $\mu$ is said to be concentrated on $C$.

Even though the word “discrete” typically has topological connotations, notice that the definition of a discrete measure does not involve any sort of topological considerations.

If $\mu$ is discrete, concentrated on $C$, and $\iota$ is the inclusion \eqref{inc-eqn}, then by the change-of-variables formula we have

\begin{equation}\label{density-off-eqn} \mu(E) = \int_E f \dx{\gamma_C} = \sum_{x\in E\cap C} f(x) \end{equation}

for all $E\in \mfm$. In particular, if each singleton $\{x\}$ is measurable (for all $x\in \calx$), then

\begin{equation}\notag \mu(\{x\}) = \begin{cases} f(x) & : x \in C, \\ 0 & : x\notin C. \end{cases} \end{equation}

In this case, we may write $\mu(x)$ in place of $f(x)$. Notice also from \eqref{density-off-eqn} that the values of $f$ on the complement $C^c$ are irrelevant as far as the measure $\mu$ is concerned; in particular, we may assume that $f(x) = 0$ for all $x\in C^c$, while still retaining the fundamental equation $\mu(E) = \int_E f\dx{\mu}$. In this case, if all singletons are measurable, we have $\mu(x) = f(x)$ for all $x\in \calx$.

Also, if $g:\calx \to \bbr$ is measurable, we have

\begin{equation}\label{int-disc-eqn} \int_\calx g \dx{\mu} = \int_\calx fg \dx{\gamma_C} = \int_C (f\circ \iota)(g\circ \iota) \dx{\gamma} = \sum_{x\in C} f(x)g(x). \end{equation}

Definition. Let $\mu$ be a measure on $(\bbr^n,\mfb(\bbr^n))$. We shall say $\mu$ is absolutely continuous if there exists a measurable function $f:\bbr^n \to [0,\infty)$ such that \begin{equation}\notag \mu(E) = \int_E f(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \ \text{d}x_1\cdots \text{d}x_n \end{equation} for all $E\in \mfb(\bbr^n)$. In this case, $f$ is called a density function of $\mu$.

Densities of random variables

Definition. Let $X$ be a random variable on a probability space $(\Omega,\mfm,P)$ and let $P_X = X_\ast P$ be the pushforward measure.

  1. We shall say $X$ is discrete if $P_X$ is a discrete measure on $\bbr$.
  2. We shall say $X$ is absolutely continuous if $P_X$ is an absolutely continuous measure on $\bbr$.

In either case, a density function $f$ of $P_X$ is called a density function of $X$.

Suppose $X$ is a discrete random variable on a probability space $(\Omega,\mfm,P)$ such that $P_X$ is concentrated on a countable Borel set $C\in \mfb(\bbr)$ with density function $f$. Then by \eqref{int-disc-eqn} we have

\begin{equation}\notag \be(X) = \int_\Omega X \dx{P} = \int_\bbr x \ P_X(\text{d}x) = \sum_{x\in C}x f(x). \end{equation}

Definition. Let $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ be a finite sequence of random variables on a probability space $(\Omega,\mfm,P)$ and let

\begin{equation}\notag P_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)} = (X_1,\ldots,X_n)_\ast P \end{equation}

be the pushforward measure.

  1. We shall say $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are jointly discrete if $P_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)}$ is a discrete measure on $\bbr^n$.
  2. We shall say $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are jointly absolutely continuous if $P_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)}$ is an absolutely continuous measure on $\bbr^n$.

In either case, a density function $f$ of $P_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)}$ is called a joint density function of the sequence $X_1,\ldots,X_n$.

Expectations

Let $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ be a finite sequence of random variables on a probability space $(\Omega,\mfm,P)$. Let

\begin{equation}\notag h:(\bbr^n, \mfb(\bbr^n)) \to (\bbr,\mfb(\bbr)) \end{equation}

be a measurable function and consider the transformed random variable $h(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$. Note that this latter random variable is actually the composite

\begin{equation}\notag \Omega \xrightarrow{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)} \bbr^n \xrightarrow{h} \bbr, \end{equation}

where the first map is defined by

\begin{equation}\notag (X_1,\ldots,X_n)(\omega) = (X_1(\omega),\ldots,X_n(\omega)) \end{equation}

for each $\omega \in \Omega$. If the random variables are jointly absolutely continuous with density $f$, then by the change-of-variables formula we have

\begin{align} \be\{h(X_1,\ldots,X_n)\} &= \int_\Omega h(X_1,\ldots,X_n) \dx{P} \notag \\ &= \int_{\bbr^n} h(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \ P_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)}(\text{d}x_1,\ldots,\text{d}x_n) \notag \\ &= \int_{\bbr^n} h(x_1,\ldots,x_n) f(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \ \text{d}x_1\cdots \text{d}x_n. \notag \end{align}

Similarly, if the random variables are jointly discrete, concentrated on the countable Borel set $C\in \mfb(\bbr^n)$ with density $f$, then we have

\begin{equation}\notag \be\{h(X_1,\ldots,X_n)\} = \sum_{(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\in C} h(x_1,\ldots,x_n) f(x_1,\ldots,x_n). \end{equation}

  1. Just to be clear, the counting measure $\gamma_C$ concentrated on $C$ is a measure on the measurable space $(\calx,\mfm)$. ↩
